fields.description:
# ACLI subprocess 429 handling: honor Retry-After if available, else jittered backoff

## Context / corrected premise
All ACLI calls go through `_run_acli` (`acli_subprocess.py`), which retries ALL non-zero exit codes (except
401/assignee) with a uniform `time.sleep(2**(attempt+1))` (2s/4s). The app-layer `_call_with_retry`
(`dispatch_one.py:100-144`) adds 429/5xx classification (delays [1,2,4]). `_call_with_backoff`
(`acli_subprocess.py:236-292`) is DEAD (zero production call sites; decoy from closed bug
baldheaded-continuous-furseal) AND operates on `urllib`/HTTP objects, NOT the subprocess `CalledProcessError`
domain â€” so it CANNOT be "revived" into `_run_acli` as-is. ACLI is a subprocess, so `Retry-After` availability
is UNVERIFIED.

## What / files (`acli_subprocess.py` `_run_acli`; `dispatch_one.py` `_call_with_retry`)
1. PROBE FIRST: run `acli jira workitem search` against a rate-limited/staged endpoint (JIRA_URL/USER/API_TOKEN)
   and record (a) whether `Retry-After` is in parseable stderr, (b) the exit code ACLI returns on HTTP 429, and
   (c) whether the ACLI Go binary already retries 429 INTERNALLY. Record on this ticket.
2. Implement 429 handling INLINE in the live `_run_acli` retry loop (adapted to the subprocess/`CalledProcessError`
   error domain â€” do NOT reuse the urllib-domain `_call_with_backoff`; DELETE that dead code to avoid confusion).
   Jittered exp-backoff with a MAX retry count + total-backoff ceiling (e.g. 3 retries / 60s); on ceiling-exhaustion
   surface `RetryExhaustedError` to callers (do not swallow). Honor `Retry-After` IFF the probe confirmed it (regex
   `Retry-After:\s*(\d+)`), else backoff without it (documented). If the probe shows ACLI already retries 429
   internally, the Python layer is a NO-OP for 429 (documented outcome).
3. Retryable-code decision EXPLICIT: today all non-zero codes retry uniformly. ADD-ON (recommended): the 429 branch
   REPLACES the uniform sleep for that attempt (no double-sleep), other codes unchanged. NARROW to {429,502,503}:
   a breaking change for other non-zero codes â€” requires a test of the changed behavior. Enumerate consumers:
   `acli_cli_ops.py` (6), `acli.py` (3), `acli_graph.py` (`_run` sites).
4. PRESERVE the non-idempotent-write guard (`AcliTimeoutError` is not a `TimeoutError`).
5. Observability: log rate-limit detection, backoff trigger, Retry-After honored-vs-unavailable, ceiling-exhaustion.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Probe recorded: Retry-After availability, the 429 exit code, and whether ACLI retries 429 internally (+ the Python-layer outcome for the internal-retry case).
- [ ] 429 handled INLINE in `_run_acli` (not the dead urllib-domain `_call_with_backoff`, which is deleted): jittered exp-backoff, max-retry/ceiling surfacing `RetryExhaustedError`; honors Retry-After IFF probe-confirmed; add-on mode does not double-sleep.
- [ ] Retryable-code decision documented; if narrowed, a test covers the changed non-429 behavior; existing non-429 retry behavior has a test either way.
- [ ] No-blind-retry-on-timeout guard preserved; new backoff exercised by a subprocess-level test (new case in `tests/.../test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py` â€” matches the impl location, not `test_error_taxonomy.py`).
- [ ] Rate-limit/backoff/Retry-After/ceiling events logged; decision captured in an ADR/design note.
